question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / offer.callback as payer, offer.buy == true] Can a maker, under the precondition that offer.buy == true and offer.callback is set to a contract that implements onBuy to call Midnight.supplyCollateral on behalf of the maker (using maker's pre-approved collateral), then after the callback Midnight pulls buyerAssets from offer.callback, violating the invariant that the callback must not be used to source funds from unrelated positions, causing scoped impact: offer.callback's tokens consumed while maker's collateral is also deposited in the same atomic transaction? Proof idea: callback calls supplyCollateral in onBuy, assert both collateral deposited and callback tokens pulled.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: SetterRatifier.isRatified / proof.length == 0 edge case] Can an attacker, under the precondition that a maker has ratified a root equal to hashOffer(offer) (a single-leaf tree with proof.length=0), submit any offer whose hash equals that root as a valid leaf with an empty proof, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct offer, causing scoped impact: any offer with the same hash as the root is accepted, potentially allowing replay of the exact offer across different takers? Proof idea: set root = hashOffer(offer), call isRatified with empty proof, assert it returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer = takerCallback, offer.buy == false, taker == authorized operator] Can an authorized operator acting on behalf of taker T, under the precondition that takerCallback is set to T's address (not the operator's address) and T has approved Midnight, call take() with taker=T and takerCallback=T, causing payer = T (via takerCallback) rather than the operator (msg.sender), violating the invariant that the taker's explicit payer designation must match the actual token source, causing scoped impact: T's tokens pulled even though the operator initiated the take? Proof idea: operator calls take(taker=T, takerCallback=T), assert T's balance decreases instead of operator's.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / buyerCallback != address(0) overrides default payer] Can a maker, under the precondition that offer.buy == true and offer.callback is set to a contract that has been pre-funded with loanToken by a previous legitimate interaction (e.g., a router contract), cause take() to drain the router contract's loanToken balance by designating it as payer via offer.callback, violating the invariant that intermediate contract balances must not be drainable by arbitrary offer fills, causing scoped impact: router/aggregator contract's loanToken balance drained? Proof idea: fund router contract with loanToken, maker sets offer.callback=router, fill offer, assert router balance decreases.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: liquidate() / payer confusion, no liquidatorGate] Can an unprivileged attacker, under the precondition that a market has no liquidatorGate (liquidatorGate == address(0)) and victim V has approved Midnight, call liquidate(market, collateralIndex, seizedAssets=0, repaidUnits=X, borrower=B, postMaturityMode=false, receiver=attacker, callback=V, data) to make V pay repaidUnits while attacker receives no collateral (seizedAssets=0), violating the invariant that the liquidator must receive value proportional to what they pay, causing scoped impact: V loses repaidUnits with no corresponding collateral transfer? Proof idea: call liquidate with seizedAssets=0 and callback=victim, assert victim loses loanToken with no collateral received.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer = offer.callback, offer.buy == true, reduceOnly == true] Can a maker, under the precondition that offer.reduceOnly == true (buyerCreditIncrease must be 0) and offer.callback is set to victim, cause take() to pull buyerAssets from victim even when the trade only reduces the buyer's existing debt (no new credit), violating the invariant that reduceOnly offers must not create unexpected token flows from third parties, causing scoped impact: victim pays buyerAssets for a trade that only reduces debt, not creates credit? Proof idea: set offer.reduceOnly=true, offer.callback=victim, fill offer where buyer has existing debt, assert victim pays buyerAssets.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified / root revocation race condition] Can a maker, under the precondition that they have ratified a root and a taker is about to fill an offer from that root, front-run the taker's take() transaction by calling setIsRootRatified(maker, root, false) to revoke the root, causing the taker's transaction to revert at the NotRatified check, violating the invariant that valid offers must remain fillable until expiry or cancellation, causing scoped impact: low-cost DoS where maker can selectively block fills by toggling root ratification? Proof idea: simulate front-run by revoking root before take(), assert take() reverts with NotRatified.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer confusion with ERC777 loanToken] Can a maker, under the precondition that loanToken is ERC777 and offer.callback is set to a contract that implements tokensToSend hook to reenter Midnight during the safeTransferFrom(loanToken, payer, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) call, cause reentrancy into take() or repay() with partial state already committed, violating the invariant that callbacks/multicall/reentrancy must not break invariants, causing scoped impact: accounting corruption via ERC777 send hook reentrancy during payer token pull? Proof idea: deploy ERC777 loanToken with tokensToSend hook that reenters take(), assert invariants hold after nested call.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer = buyerCallback, two safeTransferFrom calls] Can a maker, under the precondition that offer.buy == true and offer.callback is set to victim, cause take() to execute two separate safeTransferFrom calls from victim: first safeTransferFrom(loanToken, victim, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) for the fee, then safeTransferFrom(loanToken, victim, receiver, sellerAssets) for the seller payment, violating the invariant that the payer's total deduction must equal exactly buyerAssets, causing scoped impact: if receiver == address(this) (Midnight itself), the second transfer is a no-op but victim still pays, or if receiver is attacker, victim funds flow to attacker? Proof idea: fuzz receiver address including address(this), assert victim.balanceBefore - victim.balanceAfter == buyerAssets in all cases.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: SetterRatifier.isRatified / msg.sender == MIDNIGHT check] Can an attacker, under the precondition that they deploy a contract that calls SetterRatifier.isRatified directly (not through Midnight), observe that the NotMidnight() check prevents direct calls, but then craft a scenario where a malicious ratifier contract passes isRatified calls through to SetterRatifier while spoofing msg.sender, violating the invariant that ratifier must only be callable by Midnight, causing scoped impact: ratifier bypass allowing unauthorized offer fills? Proof idea: attempt direct call to isRatified, assert NotMidnight() reverts; then test if any indirect path can spoof msg.sender.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer confusion across markets via multicall] Can an attacker, under the precondition that victim V has approved Midnight for loanToken in market M1, use multicall to atomically call take() in market M2 (different loanToken) with offer.callback=V, causing V's onBuy to be called with M2's market parameters while V's approval is for M1's loanToken, and if V's onBuy implementation does not validate the market parameter, cause V to return CALLBACK_SUCCESS and then have Midnight pull M2's loanToken from V (if V also approved M2's loanToken), violating the invariant that callbacks must validate market context, causing scoped impact: victim's tokens in unintended market drained? Proof idea: victim approves multiple loanTokens, attacker uses victim as callback in wrong market, assert victim loses tokens in unexpected market.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer = takerCallback, offer.buy == false, taker authorized for maker] Can an attacker who is authorized for both maker M and taker T, under the precondition that T has approved Midnight, call take() with offer.buy=false (M is seller), taker=T, takerCallback=T, causing payer=T (T pays as both taker and payer), while M receives sellerAssets as seller, violating the invariant that the taker must pay from their own funds or a consenting callback, causing scoped impact: T's tokens used to fund a trade where M profits as seller? Proof idea: attacker authorized for both M and T, calls take with takerCallback=T, assert T loses buyerAssets and M gains sellerAssets.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / offer.callback in hashOffer vs payer resolution] Can a maker, under the precondition that they ratify a root via SetterRatifier containing an offer with offer.callback=address(0) (no callback, payer=buyer=maker), later have an attacker submit a modified offer struct with offer.callback=victim to take(), where the Merkle proof still validates because hashOffer is computed from the offer passed to isRatified (which includes offer.callback), causing the proof to fail for the modified offer, confirming that offer.callback is bound by the Merkle proof? Proof idea: modify offer.callback in take() call, assert isRatified reverts with InvalidProof, confirming callback binding.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / sellerCallback after token pull, liquidation lock] Can a seller (maker in sell offer with offer.callback set), under the precondition that the liquidation lock prevents liquidation of seller during callbacks, use the onSell callback to call repay() with callback=victim to make victim pay for the seller's debt repayment while the seller's liquidation is locked, violating the invariant that the liquidation lock must not enable additional fund extraction from third parties, causing scoped impact: victim pays seller's debt repayment while seller is protected from liquidation? Proof idea: seller's onSell callback calls repay(callback=victim), assert victim loses loanToken during seller's liquidation-locked window.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer confusion with zero units (no-op take)] Can an attacker, under the precondition that offer.buy == true, offer.callback=victim, and units=0, call take() to trigger IBuyCallback(victim).onBuy() with buyerAssets=0 and then safeTransferFrom(loanToken, victim, address(this), 0) and safeTransferFrom(loanToken, victim, receiver, 0), violating the invariant that no-op takes must not invoke callbacks on third-party addresses without their consent, causing scoped impact: victim's onBuy is called with zero amounts, potentially triggering unintended side effects in victim's callback logic? Proof idea: call take with units=0 and offer.callback=victim, assert victim.onBuy is called and victim's state may be corrupted.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified / root ratification for expired offers] Can a maker, under the precondition that they ratify a root containing offers with offer.expiry in the past, later have an attacker attempt to fill those offers, confirming that the OfferExpired() check in take() prevents fills regardless of root ratification status, but also test whether setIsRootRatified can be called with roots containing offers that have offer.callback=victim to pre-position a future attack when offer.expiry is in the future, violating the invariant that expired offers must not be fillable, causing scoped impact: confirm expiry check is the last defense against stale ratified roots with malicious callbacks? Proof idea: ratify root with future expiry and callback=victim, warp to expiry+1, attempt take, assert OfferExpired reverts.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer = buyerCallback, settlement fee accounting] Can a maker, under the precondition that offer.buy == true, offer.callback=victim, and settlement fee is non-zero, cause take() to pull buyerAssets (which includes the settlement fee) from victim, where claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] increases by buyerAssets - sellerAssets, violating the invariant that settlement fees must be funded by the legitimate buyer not a third party, causing scoped impact: victim funds the settlement fee that accrues to the protocol fee claimer? Proof idea: set non-zero settlement fee, offer.callback=victim, fill offer, assert claimableSettlementFee increases and victim pays the fee portion.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer confusion, offer.buy == false, takerCallback == offer.maker] Can a taker, under the precondition that offer.buy == false (maker is seller) and taker sets takerCallback = offer.maker, cause payer = offer.maker (the seller), so the seller pays buyerAssets from their own balance to fund the buyer's side of the trade, effectively making the seller pay both sides, violating the invariant that the buyer must fund buyerAssets, causing scoped impact: maker/seller's loanToken balance drained to fund the taker's purchase? Proof idea: taker sets takerCallback=offer.maker, assert offer.maker loses buyerAssets in addition to providing sellerAssets.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer = buyerCallback, onBuy receives buyer not payer] Can a maker, under the precondition that offer.buy == true and offer.callback is set to a contract that implements onBuy and uses the buyer parameter (not msg.sender) to determine how much to approve or transfer, cause a mismatch where onBuy is called with buyer=offer.maker but tokens are pulled from offer.callback (the callback contract itself), violating the invariant that the callback's buyer parameter must match the actual payer, causing scoped impact: callback contract's own tokens drained because it misidentifies the payer as buyer? Proof idea: deploy callback that approves Midnight based on buyer parameter, set offer.callback to it, fill offer, assert callback's tokens pulled not buyer's.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer confusion, post-maturity, offer.buy == true] Can a maker, under the precondition that block.timestamp > offer.market.maturity and offer.buy == true (buyer is maker, seller is taker), set offer.callback=victim to cause take() to pull buyerAssets from victim post-maturity, where sellerDebtIncrease must be 0 (CannotIncreaseDebtPostMaturity), but the payer confusion still allows victim's tokens to be drained, violating the invariant that post-maturity trading must not create new unauthorized token flows, causing scoped impact: victim's tokens drained in post-maturity unwinding trade? Proof idea: warp past maturity, fill buy offer with callback=victim, assert victim loses buyerAssets post-maturity.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified / isAuthorized[maker][ratifier] check in take()] Can an attacker, under the precondition that maker has authorized SetterRatifier as their ratifier (isAuthorized[maker][SetterRatifier]=true) and attacker has also been authorized by maker (isAuthorized[maker][attacker]=true), call setIsRootRatified(maker, maliciousRoot, true) as the attacker, then call take() with an offer from maliciousRoot where offer.callback=victim, violating the invariant that isAuthorized[maker][ratifier] must only authorize the ratifier to validate offers the maker intended, causing scoped impact: attacker exploits maker's ratifier authorization to ratify and fill malicious offers? Proof idea: maker authorizes both SetterRatifier and attacker, attacker ratifies malicious root, fills offer with callback=victim.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer confusion, maxAssets-based offer consumption] Can a maker, under the precondition that offer.maxAssets > 0 and offer.buy == true and offer.callback=victim, cause take() to consume offer capacity based on buyerAssets (consumed[maker][group] += buyerAssets) while pulling those buyerAssets from victim, violating the invariant that offer consumption must correspond to the actual payer's expenditure, causing scoped impact: victim pays buyerAssets that count against the maker's offer capacity, potentially exhausting the offer while victim bears the cost? Proof idea: fill maxAssets-based offer with callback=victim, assert consumed increases by buyerAssets and victim loses buyerAssets.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / takerCallback payer, offer.buy == false, enterGate check] Can a taker, under the precondition that offer.buy == false, takerCallback=victim, and the market has an enterGate that checks canIncreaseDebt(seller), cause take() to pass the gate check for the seller (maker) but pull tokens from victim (takerCallback) as payer, violating the invariant that gate checks must apply to all entry paths including payer designation, causing scoped impact: gate-restricted market allows victim's tokens to be pulled even though victim never passed the gate? Proof idea: create gated market, set takerCallback=victim (not gate-approved), fill offer, assert victim's tokens pulled despite not passing gate.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer confusion, group budget and cross-offer attack] Can a maker, under the precondition that they have multiple offers in the same group (shared consumption budget) each with offer.callback=different_victim, cause multiple takers to fill different offers in the group, each time pulling tokens from a different victim address, violating the invariant that offer group budgets must not enable multi-victim token extraction, causing scoped impact: multiple victims' tokens drained across group-linked offers up to the group's maxAssets/maxUnits budget? Proof idea: create N offers in same group each with callback=victim_i, fill all offers, assert each victim_i loses tokens and group budget is exhausted.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: repay() / callback payer, onRepay receives onBehalf not payer] Can an attacker, under the precondition that they are authorized for borrower B and victim V has approved Midnight, call repay(market, units, onBehalf=B, callback=V, data) where V's onRepay implementation uses the onBehalf parameter to determine authorization (assuming onBehalf is the payer), causing V to return CALLBACK_SUCCESS believing it is acting for B, while Midnight pulls tokens from V (not B), violating the invariant that the callback's onBehalf parameter must not be confused with the payer, causing scoped impact: V's tokens drained because V's onRepay misidentifies payer as onBehalf? Proof idea: deploy victim contract whose onRepay checks onBehalf==self, call repay with callback=victim and onBehalf=victim, assert victim loses tokens.
